_meta.disclaimer: Synthetic geological context for research demonstration. Tunnel B (anonymised Burnley) data is well-grounded in published sources for the real-world tunnel route (Paul et al. 2014 'Engineering Geological Evolution of the Jolimont Valley'; Holdgate/Cupper et al. 2003; Lamb & Hutchinson 1998; The Conversation 2025 Burnley reference). Tunnel A (anonymised West Gate) data is plausible per Melbourne regional geology (Yarra/Maribyrnong delta sequence, Coode Island Silt, Older Volcanics, Silurian Melbourne Formation basement) but the specific stratigraphic boundaries along that route are not published and are interpolated.
primary_sources: Paul et al. 2014, 'The Engineering Geological Evolution of the Jolimont Valley, Melbourne, Victoria', Australian Geomechanics 49(2)
Holdgate G.R., Cupper M.L. et al. 2003, geological evolution of the Holocene Yarra Delta
Lamb K., Hutchinson J. 1998, ground models for the Burnley Tunnel
Cupper M.L. et al. 2003, Yarra Delta sediments stratigraphy
Geological Map of Melbourne SJ 55-5 (Victorian Department of Primary Industries)

TUN-A.label: Tunnel A
TUN-A.geological_setting: Yarraville–Footscray Road corridor crossing Maribyrnong/Yarra delta sequence above Silurian basement
TUN-A.groundwater_setting: Generally below water table along bored sections; saline intrusion from estuarine system
TUN-A.data_provenance: Plausible per Melbourne regional geology. Specific stratigraphic boundaries along this route are interpolated from regional geological mapping.

zones_along_chainage.name: Western portal — recent fill and Coode Island Silt
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Engineered fill over Coode Island Silt
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Coode Island Silt (CIS) — soft to firm grey silty clay, marine origin
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Above water table at portal level after dewatering. Diaphragm wall support standard.

zones_along_chainage.name: Western bored — Yarra Delta sediments and weathered basalt
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Coode Island Silt grading into Port Melbourne Sand; Newer Volcanics basalt at depth
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Mixed-face: Port Melbourne Sand (loose to medium dense) overlying weathered basalt
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: TBM operated through mixed-face conditions (sand/clay/weathered basalt). Annular grouting at 2.5–3.5 bar.

zones_along_chainage.name: Central deep — Silurian Melbourne Formation
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Silurian Melbourne Formation (interbedded siltstone and sandstone)
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Slightly weathered to fresh Silurian mudstone/siltstone; locally fractured
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Below water table along full length. Saline groundwater (estuarine zone). Higher cover and corrosion protection specified due to saline exposure. Annular grout at 3–4 bar.

zones_along_chainage.name: Eastern bored — Yarra Delta sediments returning
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Yarra Delta sediments — Coode Island Silt and clays over weathered basalt
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Mostly Coode Island Silt; transitions to mixed face toward eastern portal
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: TBM advance through silts and clays; ground conditions improve toward portal.

zones_along_chainage.name: Eastern portal — recent fill and silts
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Engineered fill over recent alluvium / Coode Island Silt
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Cut-and-cover. Above water table after dewatering during construction.

TUN-B.label: Tunnel B
TUN-B.geological_setting: Southbank–Burnley corridor crossing Yarra Delta sediments and entering deep Silurian Melbourne Formation under the Yarra River; intersects the Jolimont Valley channel feature near Burnley
TUN-B.groundwater_setting: Below water table for ~95% of length; under Yarra River the deep mudstone section faces hydrostatic head exceeding 6 bar at maximum depth
TUN-B.data_provenance: Well-grounded in Paul et al. 2014, Holdgate/Cupper et al. 2003, Lamb & Hutchinson 1998, and the Conversation Burnley reference. Stratigraphic units and approximate depths reflect the published cross-sections of the real tunnel route.

zones_along_chainage.name: Western portal — Coode Island Silt / Port Melbourne Sand sequence
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Yarra Delta sediments — Coode Island Silt and Port Melbourne Sand
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Port Melbourne Sand at tunnel level; Coode Island Silt as overburden
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Top-down secant pile construction. Working through CIS / PMS sequence. Port Melbourne Sand acts as a confined aquifer under the silt — dewatering required.

zones_along_chainage.name: Yarra crossing approach — Moray Street Gravels and weathered basalt
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Moray Street Gravels (sand/gravel aquifer ~20m thick) above Burnley Basalt and Older Volcanics
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Weathered basalt grading into fresh basalt; Moray Street Gravels above
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Roadheader excavation through weathered basalt overlying mudstone. Moray Street Gravels are a documented aquifer along this corridor (per Conversation 2025 reference; Burnley Tunnel construction encountered this aquifer requiring dewatering).
hazards: Permeable Moray Street Gravels aquifer above
Approach to Yarra crossing — gasket sealing critical

zones_along_chainage.name: Yarra crossing — deep Silurian Melbourne Formation
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Silurian Melbourne Formation (siltstone, mudstone, fine sandstone)
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Slightly weathered to fresh Melbourne Mudstone; locally fractured zones
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Maximum depth ~65 m. Below the Yarra River. Driven through Silurian mudstone (Melbourne Mudstone). Encountered unexpectedly high water pressures during construction (per Wikipedia reference) requiring grouting interventions. Hydrostatic head approaches 6.5 bar at maximum depth. Mudstone is porous — water from Moray Street Gravels aquifer above can leak into deeper formations.
hazards: Porous mudstone allowing water transmission from upper aquifer

zones_along_chainage.name: Eastern bored (Richmond) — Melbourne Formation with basalt intrusions
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Silurian Melbourne Formation with intermittent Older Volcanics basalt intrusions
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Melbourne Mudstone with locally interbedded weathered basalt dykes
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Mudstone with intermittent basalt intrusions (dykes weathered to clay locally). Standard ground conditions return. Crosses the Jolimont Valley channel feature inferred per Paul et al. 2014.

zones_along_chainage.name: Eastern portal (Burnley) — Burnley Basalt over mudstone
zones_along_chainage.primary_unit: Burnley Basalt (Newer Volcanics, ~0.81 Ma) overlying Melbourne Formation
zones_along_chainage.tunnel_substrate: Melbourne Mudstone; Burnley Basalt as cap
zones_along_chainage.engineering_notes: Top-down cut-and-cover. Burnley Basalt (Newer Volcanics, ~0.81 Ma per Page 1968) caps the formation. Above water table for most of the cut-and-cover section after dewatering.
